title: Add support for sqlite/tursodb
body: Would be great to have durable execution in even more lightweight scenarios

title: Better Serialization Errors
body: Currently, `jsonpickle` fails silently if it can't serialize an object. This can cause workflows to silently become unrecoverable. We should make serialization failures more visible.

title: Asyncio
body: Add support for asynchronous functions (workflows, steps, transactions).

title: Make cron spec available for scheduled workflows
body:
Requested by a user in the off-topic discord channel:

> Hi - not sure where else to put it. When I specify a scheduled workflow, the function is added to the DBOS system database as job_queue, The cron specification, however, is not available in the system database as property of a workflow (or job). For debugging reasons, esp. in case of time travel, that might come in handy as the specification might be important to interpret the workflow_status.

title: Invalid cron spec does not trigger an error
body:
From a discord conversation https://discord.com/channels/1156433345631232100/1217220112570912898/1290004975757557761

```
@DBOS.scheduled("*/invalid * * * * *") but does not throw an error, either. And its invalid, just checked with an online cron format validator.
```

title: Make admin server port configurable
body:
Currently, the admin server always runs on port 3001.
However, users may want to change it due to some conflicts in their environment.

title: limit workeflow_inputs table growth?
body: It may be part of DBA but I'd like to know if dbos already supports workflow_inputs regular cleanup and flush. Or is there a switch to turn off the store explicitly?

title: Scheduled workflow does not recover from database connection outage?
body:
Hi - please see the following code and log output. The scheduled workflow does not seem to recover from a database connection outage. The below log shows the application running, then I manually restart the database. This causes the various stacktraces in the log. At the very end of the log file the application recovered, but the scheduled workflows did not.

This is latest DBOS Python 0.11 version and Postgres in a container.

My expectation was that DBOS recovers completely, including scheduled workflows, surviving a database outage without any external recovery mechanism (that would have to be recoverable as well).

Do you have any suggestion how to handle this case? Did I miss any parameter or configuration setting to enable it?

Thank you.

```
import sys
import time
import traceback
from datetime import datetime

from dbos import DBOS

DBOS()


@DBOS.scheduled("*/5 * * * * *")
@DBOS.workflow()
def print_log(scheduled_time: datetime,
              actual_time: datetime) -> None:
    DBOS.logger.info("Workflow ran")


def main(arguments: list[str]) -> None:
    DBOS().launch()

    while True:
        time.sleep(5)
        DBOS.logger.info('alive')


if __name__ == "__main__":
    try:
        main(sys.argv)
    except KeyboardInterrupt:
        DBOS.logger.warning('KeyboardInterrupt received')
    except Exception as e:
        DBOS.logger.error(traceback.format_exc())
    finally:
        DBOS.destroy()

```

title: Invalid workflow schedule exception (a) cannot be caught (b) does not prevent attempt of execution
body:
Hi - based on [https://github.com/dbos-inc/dbos-transact-py/issues/123](https://github.com/dbos-inc/dbos-transact-py/issues/123) I tried to catch the exception and terminate my DBOS application (code and log below).

1. I was not able to catch the exception as result of `DBOS.launch()`. While an error log entry was written, the exception does not seem to bubble up as a result of `DBOS.launch()`.

1. As a side effect you can observe that DBOS nevertheless tries to start the workflow (even though it has an invalid schedule).

My expectation was that if a workflow specification is incorrect, that `DBOS.launch()` throws an exception (since the incorrect specification is known) and not continue its operation. 

Please see code and log below, this is the Python 0.11 version.

```
import sys
import time
import traceback
from datetime import datetime

from dbos import DBOS

DBOS()


@DBOS.scheduled("*/invalid * * * * *")
@DBOS.workflow()
def print_log(scheduled_time: datetime,
              actual_time: datetime) -> None:
    DBOS.logger.info("Workflow cannot run")


def main(arguments: list[str]) -> None:
    try:
        DBOS().launch()
    except Exception:
        DBOS.logger.error("launch error")
        sys.exit(-1)

    while True:
        time.sleep(5)
        DBOS.logger.info('alive')


if __name__ == "__main__":
    try:
        main(sys.argv)
    except KeyboardInterrupt:
        DBOS.logger.warning('KeyboardInterrupt received')
    except Exception as e:
        DBOS.logger.error(traceback.format_exc())
    finally:
        DBOS.destroy()
```

title: Scheduled workflow raising exception: where to observe re-execution?
body:
The code below shows a scheduled workflow that raises an exception on first execution. In order to identify the executing workflow a log statement logs the workflow id (`sched-print_log-2024-11-05T03:31:50+00:00`).

When another workflow is scheduled, it will not raise an exception due to the `workflow_failed` Boolean variable set to `True` when the first workflow raises an exception.

My expectation was that the failed workflow with id `sched-print_log-2024-11-05T03:31:50+00:00` will be re-executed as it did not successfully complete on first execution. Where in the DBOS schema can I observe that the failed workflow is re-executed and successful on 2nd try?

Thank you.

```
import sys
import time
import traceback
from datetime import datetime

from dbos import DBOS

DBOS()

workflow_failed: bool = False


@DBOS.scheduled("*/10 * * * * *")
@DBOS.workflow()
def print_log(scheduled_time: datetime,
              actual_time: datetime) -> None:
    DBOS.logger.info(f"Workflow with id {DBOS.workflow_id} start ...")
    global workflow_failed
    if not workflow_failed:
        workflow_failed = True
        raise Exception()
    DBOS.logger.info(f"... workflow with id {DBOS.workflow_id} completed")


def main(arguments: list[str]) -> None:
    DBOS().launch()

    while True:
        time.sleep(5)
        DBOS.logger.info('alive')


if __name__ == "__main__":
    try:
        main(sys.argv)
    except KeyboardInterrupt:
        DBOS.logger.warning('KeyboardInterrupt received')
    except Exception as e:
        DBOS.logger.error(traceback.format_exc())
    finally:
        DBOS.destroy()
```

title: Startup of DBOS fails when database is unavailable at startup time
body:
Thank you for addressing recovering from a database outage in https://github.com/dbos-inc/dbos-transact-py/issues/139

I admit that I was secretly hoping that this will also support starting up DBOS when the database is unavailable. However, currently (python 0.12) DBOS exits when started with the database unavailable (see reproduction below, start the code with database down, log attached).

My expectation was that I can start DBOS without the database being available as well. So if the database is available or not, start, execution, and stop of DBOS shall be possible (the life cycle of both, DBOS and database are independent of each other).

The underlying rationale is that DBOS, database (and possibly other components) are deployed on VMs or containers that are independent of each other in terms of their life cycle. It might be possible that the database is restarted (or moved in an HA/DR - high availability/disaster recovery - case, #139 addresses this). It might be possible that the database is running fine, but the container/VM that runs DBOS is crashing or moved due to HA/DR or other reasons. Both might be happening concurrently in an HA/DR case. If all systems are restarted, then ideally I do not want to introduce an order of restart between the VMs/containers in order to reduce complexity and avoid deadlock. Ideally the infrastructure restart of VMs/containers is not dictated by the applications those are running.

Thank you.

import sys
import time

from datetime import datetime

from dbos import DBOS

DBOS()

@DBOS.workflow()

title: Recovery from exception during launch fails
body:
In the application code below DBOS is launched with one workflow. The script is implemented so that it catches exceptions during DBOS.launch() and retries when an exception is raised with the goal to get to a normally running system.

To trigger an exception I use the database being available (or not) during launch.

- Database available: all works find, see first log below after the application code. 

- Database unavailable and made available afterwards: exceptions are raised and logged. Once the database is available, you can observe in the log (last part) that the application is running, however, the workflow is not launching.

The expected behavior is that the launch can survive exceptions and the system gets back to a consistent state after the exception is resolved.

Could you please have a look if the exception handling is correct or if a change would get the system to a running state?

Thank you.

(DBOS Python 0.13, Python 3.12)

Application code:

```
import sys
import time
import traceback
from datetime import datetime

from dbos import DBOS

DBOS()


@DBOS.scheduled("*/5 * * * * *")
@DBOS.workflow()
def print_log(scheduled_time: datetime,
              actual_time: datetime) -> None:
    DBOS.logger.info(f"Workflow ran")


def main(arguments: list[str]) -> None:
    successful_launch: bool = False
    while not successful_launch:
        try:
            DBOS().launch()
            successful_launch = True
        except Exception as ex:
            DBOS.logger.error(f"Exception raised during DBOS.launch(): {ex}")
            DBOS.destroy()
            time.sleep(5)

    while True:
        time.sleep(5)
        DBOS.logger.info('alive')


if __name__ == "__main__":
    try:
        main(sys.argv)
    except KeyboardInterrupt:
        DBOS.logger.warning('KeyboardInterrupt received')
    except Exception as e:
        DBOS.logger.error(traceback.format_exc())
    finally:
        DBOS.destroy()
```

title: Route Confusion
body:
Analogous to https://github.com/dbos-inc/dbos-transact-ts/issues/666 but more consistent. 

If we do something like this:
```python
@app.get("/greeting/theotherone")
def greeting_one() -> str:
    greeting = f"Called the other greeting"
    DBOS.logger.info(greeting)
    return greeting

@app.get("/greeting/{name}")
def greeting_one(name: str) -> str:
    greeting = f"Called greeting with {name}"
    DBOS.logger.info(greeting)
    return greeting
```

The first method declared in the file will get precedence.

title: DBOS.destroy() exhibits different behavior from DBOS._destroy()
body:
Hi, the following script behaves differently when using `DBOS.destroy()` and `DBOS._destroy()`. For my use case, `_destroy()` is the desired behavior.

`DBOS._destroy()`
1. Start the script with the database down
2. Observe exceptions
3. Start the database
4. Observe that (a) exceptions are not raised anymore and (b) that the specified workflow is executing

`DBOS.destroy()` (no `_`)
1. Start the script with the database down
2. Observe exceptions
3. Start the database
4. Observe that (a) exceptions are not raised anymore, however, (b) the specified workflow is *not* executing (but should)

My expectation is that `destroy()` and `_destroy()` have the same behavior, whereby `_destroy()` is the desired one for my use case.

```
import sys
import time
import traceback

from DateTime import DateTime  # type: ignore
from dbos import DBOS


@DBOS.scheduled("*/5 * * * * *")
@DBOS.workflow()
def print_log(scheduled_time: DateTime,
              actual_time: DateTime) -> None:
    DBOS.logger.info(f"Workflow ran")


def main(arguments: list[str]) -> None:
    dbos: DBOS | None = None
    dbos_launched = False
    while not dbos_launched:
        # noinspection PyBroadException
        try:
            dbos: DBOS = DBOS()
            DBOS.logger.info('DBOS launching')
            DBOS().launch()
            dbos_launched = True
        except Exception:
            DBOS.logger.error(traceback.format_exc())
            dbos._destroy()
            # dbos.destroy()
            time.sleep(5)
            dbos_launched = False
    # end while

    while True:
        time.sleep(5)
        DBOS.logger.info('alive')


if __name__ == "__main__":
    try:
        main(sys.argv)
    except KeyboardInterrupt:
        DBOS.logger.warning('KeyboardInterrupt received')
    except Exception as e:
        DBOS.logger.error(traceback.format_exc())
    finally:
        DBOS.destroy()
```

title: DBOS.start_workflow and async workflows
body:
When you have async workflows but start them using `DBOS.start_workflow(...)`, calls to `await` in the workflow can hang.

I believe this is because you should use:

```python
async def some_dbos_implementation_function():
    executor = ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=3)
    asyncio.get_event_loop().run_in_executor(executor, blocking_io_task, arg)
```

instead of:

```python
async def incorrect_approach():
    executor = ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=3)
    result = executor.submit(blocking_io_task, 5).result()  # Blocks here!
    return result
```

Note, I'm not an asyncio internals expert, but [this is what Claude told me](https://claude.ai/share/7bc58613-b228-49df-a366-99b219afb297) while debugging.


Here is sample code that demonstrates the problem:


```python
from contextlib import asynccontextmanager
from typing import AsyncGenerator
import uuid

from dbos import DBOS, SetWorkflowID
from fastapi import FastAPI
from playwright.async_api import async_playwright, Browser, Playwright


class BrowserWrapper:
    _browser: Browser | None = None

    @classmethod
    async def get_browser(cls) -> Browser:
        if cls._browser is None:
            raise RuntimeError("Browser not initialized")
        return cls._browser

    @classmethod
    async def init(cls, pw: Playwright) -> Browser:
        b = await pw.chromium.launch()
        cls._browser = b
        return b


@asynccontextmanager
async def fastapi_lifespan(
    app_: FastAPI,  # pylint: disable=unused-argument
) -> AsyncGenerator[None, None]:
    """FastAPI lifespan context manager

    The part before the yield runs at app startup, and the part after runs at
    app shutdown.
    """
    async with async_playwright() as pw:
        await BrowserWrapper.init(pw)
        yield
    yield


app = FastAPI(lifespan=fastapi_lifespan)

@DBOS.workflow()
async def wf() -> int:
    browser = await BrowserWrapper.get_browser()
    return await step(browser)


@DBOS.step()
async def step(browser: Browser) -> int:
    print(f"the step started {DBOS.workflow_id}")
    ctx = await browser.new_context(
        viewport={"width": 1920, "height": 1080},
        user_agent="hi",
        screen={"width": 1920, "height": 1080},
    )
    # this will never print when you call DBOS.start_workflow(wf)
    print(f"the context finished {DBOS.workflow_id}")
    return 42


@app.get("/simple")
async def simple() -> str:
    # When using `DBOS.start_workflow(wf)`, we get blocked by an await call
    with SetWorkflowID(str(uuid.uuid4())):
        handle = DBOS.start_workflow(wf)
    return handle.get_workflow_id()

    # change it to an await call and it finishes
    # with SetWorkflowID(str(uuid.uuid4())):
    #     await wf()
    # return "hello"


DBOS(fastapi=app)
```

title: Make child workflows create OpenTelemetry child spans
body:
Copy the original discussions from https://github.com/dbos-inc/dbos-transact-py/pull/317 (@dbmikus)

Make child workflows create OpenTelemetry child spans so that you can track execution across sub-workflows.

Testing:

- tested running against a local workflow that creates child works

Issues to fix:

- `Queue("...").enqueue_async` workflows do not create child spans

When I create child workflows via like so:

```
myqueue = Queue("myqueue", concurrency=25)

@DBOS.workflow()
async def wf():
    await myqueue.enqueue_async(sub_wf)

@DBOS.workflow()
async def sub_wf():
    pass
```

the `sub_wf` workflows show up as new traces.

It would be useful to use standard OTel tooling for tracking workflows in DBOS, if possible.

I understand that there are pain points with OTel and very long-running traces. I've previously put traces on Kafka and SQS, but those were consumed relatively quickly. TBH, I'm not sure of the ramifications of having a trace that can exist for days. There might be no problems, or it might break OTel collection. There are [ways to link two traces together](https://opentelemetry.io/docs/concepts/signals/traces/#span-links), which might alleviate long-lived trace problems.

Another simpler solution is to make child workflows exist within the same trace as long as they are not executed on a different executor.


For context, we use OpenTelemetry for observability and sometimes data collection of our LLMs. We record some function inputs/outputs on OTel spans and also record log messages in the spans. Being able to debug the LLM flow across spans is very helpful, and other LLM ops products support ingesting OTel traces.

title: DBOS Client should not run migrations
body: Currently, DBOS Client uses the system DB, but it'll try to create tables if not exist. However, this is not ideal when the client and server have different DBOS versions. We don't want the client to modify the system tables.

title: Consistently update `updated_at` for workflow status
body:
Currently, we update `updated_at` in `insert_workflow_status` and `update_workflow_status`, but we don't update this field in `cancel_workflow` or `resume_workflow` or other places.

We should consistently update `updated_at` across all operations.

Another issue is that if a workflow has completed but a later request invokes it again, the `updated_at` field will be updated even if the workflow doesn't run at all. We shouldn't update it if the workflow has completed.

title: db_retry can cause oversleep
body:
The end time should be computed ahead of time instead of in the `sleep` function: https://github.com/dbos-inc/dbos-transact-py/blob/main/dbos/_sys_db.py#L1521-L1544

If the database is temporarily down, then this sleep might take much longer.

title: Workflow hanging on `get_result` cancellation
body:
```python
workflow_handle = await DBOS.start_workflow_async(ingest_document)
document_id = await workflow_handle.get_result()
```

Not sure if this is intended behavior, but I have the following lines in a FastAPI endpoint, and when the request gets disconnected, and I await `get_result`, my workflow just hangs.

However, I expect my workflow to continue processing in the background. This happens because when the request gets disconnected, asyncio cancels the `get_result` future, and somehow that messes things up with DBOS. 

My temporary solution is to wrap `get_result` with `asyncio.shield`, and this works, but maybe DBOS should internally handle `get_result` cancellations. 

```python
workflow_handle = await DBOS.start_workflow_async(ingest_document)
document_id = await asyncio.shield(workflow_handle.get_result())
```

`dbos==1.9.0`

title: Implement DBOS.run_step for in-line steps
body:
Currently, DBOS Python has to use the `@DBOS.step` decorator if we want to execute a function as a step.
In DBOS TypeScript, we have a powerful [`DBOS.runStep`](https://docs.dbos.dev/typescript/reference/workflows-steps#dbosrunstep) function, allowing users to run a function as a step (even an inline function) in a workflow without decoration.

Would be a nice feature to add to Python.

title: Question:  How can i do an async fanout?
body:
I want to implement a fanout/gather pattern similar to [Inngest's fan-out jobs](https://www.inngest.com/docs/guides/fan-out-jobs)(https://www.inngest.com/docs/guides/fan-out-jobs) where I can:
1. Trigger multiple async operations in parallel from a single workflow
2. Collect all results when they complete

something like this the 2 examples beow


## Example 1: Same operation for N items

```python
@DBOS.workflow()
async def process_batch_workflow(items):
    # Fanout - process each item in parallel
    handles = []
    for item in items:
        handle = process_item.start_async(item)
        handles.append(handle)
    
    # Gather - collect all results
    results = await asyncio.gather(*[
        handle.get_result_async() for handle in handles
    ])
    
    return results

@DBOS.step()
async def process_item(item):
    # Process single item asynchronously
    async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:
        response = await session.post("https://api.example.com/process", json=item)
        return await response.json()
```

however each step can only run once right for example 1?

title: Feature Request: Custom Database Driver Plugin System
body:
# Summary

Add support for pluggable custom database drivers to allow organizations with specialized database infrastructure to integrate their custom connection management, credential handling, and database interaction patterns.

# Problem Statement

Our organization uses a managed PostgreSQL platform with custom credential management, connection pooling, and security requirements that differ from standard PostgreSQL connections. The current DBOS implementation hardcodes the PostgreSQL driver (`postgresql+psycopg`) and connection logic, making it difficult to integrate with our internal platform team's database infrastructure.

We need a way to plug in our custom database driver that handles:

  - Custom credential management and rotation
  - Specialized connection settings and security requirements
  - Internal monitoring and observability integration
  - Platform-specific connection pooling strategies

This would of course need to be replicated pattern in other languages, however the primary use case I currently have is python and golang.

# Proposed Solution

## 1. Database Driver Interface

Create an abstract base class that custom drivers can implement:

```python
  from abc import ABC, abstractmethod
  import sqlalchemy as sa
  from typing import Dict, Any

  class DatabaseDriver(ABC):
      @abstractmethod
      def create_engine(self, database_url: str, engine_kwargs: Dict[str, Any]) -> sa.Engine:
          """Create and return a configured SQLAlchemy engine."""
          pass

      @abstractmethod
      def validate_url(self, database_url: str) -> bool:
          """Validate that the provided URL is compatible with this driver."""
          pass

      @abstractmethod
      def get_dialect_name(self) -> str:
          """Return the SQLAlchemy dialect name for this driver."""
          pass
```

## 2. Driver Registry System

Implement a registry system similar to the existing custom PostgreSQL implementation pattern:

```python
  class DatabaseDriverRegistry:
      _drivers: Dict[str, DatabaseDriver] = {}

      @classmethod
      def register_driver(cls, name: str, driver: DatabaseDriver) -> None:
          """Register a custom database driver."""
          cls._drivers[name] = driver

      @classmethod
      def get_driver(cls, name: str) -> Optional[DatabaseDriver]:
          """Retrieve a registered driver by name."""
          return cls._drivers.get(name)
```

## 3. Configuration Integration

Extend the existing configuration system to support custom drivers:

```yaml
  # dbos-config.yaml
  name: my-app
  system_database_url: "custom://internal-postgres"
  database:
    custom_driver: "internal_postgres_driver"
```

```python
  # DBOSConfig type extension
  class DBOSConfig(TypedDict, total=False):
      # ... existing fields ...
      custom_database_driver: Optional[str]
```

## 4. Usage Example

Organizations could implement their custom driver:

```python
  from dbos import DatabaseDriver, DatabaseDriverRegistry
  import sqlalchemy as sa

  class InternalPostgresDriver(DatabaseDriver):
      def create_engine(self, database_url: str, engine_kwargs: Dict[str, Any]) -> sa.Engine:
          # Custom credential fetching
          credentials = self._fetch_internal_credentials()

          # Transform URL with internal connection details
          internal_url = self._transform_to_internal_url(database_url, credentials)

          # Apply internal connection settings
          internal_kwargs = {**engine_kwargs, **self._get_internal_engine_config()}

          return sa.create_engine(internal_url, **internal_kwargs)

      def validate_url(self, database_url: str) -> bool:
          return database_url.startswith("custom://")

      def get_dialect_name(self) -> str:
          return "postgresql"  # Still PostgreSQL, but with custom connection logic
```

### Register the driver

```python
  DatabaseDriverRegistry.register_driver("internal_postgres_driver", InternalPostgresDriver())
```

# Benefits

  1. Platform Integration: Organizations can integrate DBOS with their existing database infrastructure without forking the codebase
  2. Security Compliance: Custom drivers can implement organization-specific security requirements and credential management
  3. Observability: Internal drivers can integrate with existing monitoring and logging systems
  4. Future-Proof: Extensible system that can accommodate various database platforms and connection patterns
  5. Backward Compatibility: Existing configurations continue to work unchanged

# Implementation Notes

  - The system should fall back to default driver when no custom driver is specified.
  - Custom drivers should be loaded and registered before DBOS initialization. But ideally also load after `DBOS(...)` initialization call.
  - Error handling should provide clear feedback when custom drivers fail to load or validate
  - Documentation should include examples for common use cases (credential rotation, connection proxies like pgbouncer, pg-proxy, etc.)

# Alternative Approaches Considered

  1. Environment Variable Overrides: Limited flexibility, doesn't address complex connection logic
  2. Configuration File Templating: Doesn't handle dynamic credential management or complex connection patterns
  3. Monkey Patching: Fragile and makes upgrades difficult, although this is how I've currently hacked a proof-of-concept together.


This feature would enable organizations with specialized database infrastructure to adopt DBOS while maintaining their existing security, monitoring, and operational practices.

title: Feature Request: get_current_events
body:
When designing a frontend for a specific workflow, I want to be able to get the current status of the workflow in terms of events as a key-value JSON dict. This is useful for tracking progress and state and present it to our client.

Right now I have made an endpoint in the backend which is similar to the example below:

```python

def get_current_events(workflow_id, keys: List[str]):
    results = {}
    for key in event_keys:
        result = DBOS.get_event(workflow_id, key, timeout_seconds=0)
        # Optimally handle string conversion based on the type of `result`, like `BaseModel`.
        results[key] = result
    return results
```
    
Afaik the `get_event` method is meant to be used for asyncio processes which might include waiting (sleep), although luckily we do not bloat the database with workflows for sleeps for this call since the timeout is 0 seconds, and it's not blocking.

Cons:
* I am using a method meant for something else (waiting for values to be read/broadcast).
* I have to perform multiple queries into the database when it could be just one query.
* I have to explicitly declare which keys I want.

Proposal:

Create a method akin to
 ```python
 DBOS.get_current_events(workflow_id: str, keys: Optional[List[str]]
```

This method would do a direct query to the database and return the results, bypassing other DBOS code related to conditional workflow orchestration. If there are problems related to serialization, it could raise. 


PS:
This is something I can currently work around, so there's no rush to implement it (if it's a good idea) on my behalf.

title: [Design] `pickle` as a serialization format
body:
Hi all,

I came across DBOS while researching various AI agents framework and noticed it's integration with Pydantic AI.  You have built a very neat library that solves a meaningful business challenge that most companies end up building in-house, so kudos for that!

I wanted to raise a question that sort of bothered me w.r.t to the adoption of DBOS. While not an issue per se, I wonder if this will end up documented somehow.    

Can you elaborate on the design decision to use `pickle` as the Python serialization format, compared to JSON or other more structured binary formats?  When using DBOS (in the context of Pydantic AI, but in-directly), I peeked into the database to understand the storage model, and found it extremely surprising that the data in the internal database of DBOS is pickled.  Personally, I do consider it an adoption blocker for various usecases:
- The serialized data might potentially include results from third party sources, which can not be trusted to be unpickled. 
- Changes in data format, code layout and binary structure might prevent from deserializing a stored object in the future, which makes it unusable for long term storage.

Unless I misunderstood the docs, the TypeScript flavor is using JSON data, which is further confusing me (are those structures interchangeable?).

Would be happy to hear about the tradeoffs you've considered, and whether there are plans to phase out `pickle` in future versions towards more secure format.

title: Recommended approach to build DAG workflows
body:
Hi there,

I'm generally very hooked on the simple nature of DBOS but I'm struggling to understand what would be the correct approach to build a proper DAG workflow with the framework. DAGs with arbitrary number of levels, downstream dependencies and execution times. And by that, I mean not simply executing the DAG by level, which could be solved using multiple queues, but efficiently whenever a node is ready.

My current approach is the following: 
* Use a single queue.
* In my workflow function, my DAG resolution algo enqueues each node as a step when that node is ready.
* A node is ready when the upstream steps are finished, which currently requires me to manually poll the status for a list of handles to wait for the first one to finish.

It kinda works but doesn't feel right. It feels like an anti-pattern considering the current design of DBOS. Especially when I noticed that the `DBOS.getStatus` is registered as a step of the workflow, resulting in workflows of hundreds or thousands of steps due to polling.

It would be great to get some recommendation on how to build that. Unless it's not really supported by the framework. If it is, I think adding an example in the official documentation would also be a great addition.

title: `worker_concurrency` behaves like global `concurrency`
body:
We're trying to setup multiple workers to dequeue from the same queue as described in this DBOS [post](https://www.dbos.dev/blog/why-postgres-durable-execution). The issue is that specifying `worker_concurrency` seems to behave like global `concurrency` instead.

In this simplified example, we'd expect 2 concurrent executions (1 per worker), but they appear to execute sequentially. This is using DBOS 2.3.0.

```py3
# worker.py
import os
import time
import sys

from dbos import DBOS, DBOSConfig, Queue

WORKER_ID=int(sys.argv[1])

DBOS(
    config=DBOSConfig(
        name=f"dbos-worker-{WORKER_ID}",
        run_admin_server=False,
        system_database_url="sqlite:///dbos_worker.sqlite"
    )
)

queue = Queue("example-queue", worker_concurrency=1)

@DBOS.workflow()
def task():
    time.sleep(2)
    print(f"WORKER {WORKER_ID}: done")

if __name__ == '__main__':
    DBOS.launch()
    try:
        while True:
            time.sleep(5)
    except KeyboardInterrupt:
        DBOS.destroy()
        os._exit(0)
```

```py3
# trigger.py
from dbos import DBOSClient

client = DBOSClient(system_database_url="sqlite:///dbos_worker.sqlite")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    for _ in range(2):
        client.enqueue({
            "queue_name": "example-queue",
            "workflow_name": "task",
        })
```

Launch:
```sh
python3 worker.py 1 &
p1=$!
python3 worker.py 2 &
p2=$!
trap "kill $p1 $p2" EXIT
python3 trigger.py
wait $p1 $p2
```

Are we misunderstanding or misconfiguring something here? Thanks.

title: shouldn't check status for queued_only in get_workflows
body:
https://github.com/dbos-inc/dbos-transact-java/pull/212#discussion_r2535032924

"The status filter for queuesOnly is hardcoded to PENDING and ENQUEUED states, which conflicts with any user-specified status filter in the input. If a user provides a different status while queuesOnly is true, the query will apply both filters creating an impossible condition (e.g., status = 'SUCCESS' AND status IN ('PENDING', 'ENQUEUED')). This should either override the user's status filter or combine with it appropriately."

I had originally copied the python code for Java's `listWorkflows` function, but then removed the status check as per the above feedback.

title: Scheduler generates string-based workflow IDs instead of UUIDs
body:
## Issue

The scheduler in `_scheduler.py` creates workflow IDs using formatted strings rather than actual UUIDs, even though they're stored in a column named `workflow_uuid`.

**Current implementation** ([_scheduler.py:45-47](https://github.com/dbos-inc/dbos-transact-py/blob/main/dbos/_scheduler.py#L45-L47)):

```python
workflowID = (
    f"sched-{get_dbos_func_name(func)}-{next_exec_time.isoformat()}"
)
```

This generates IDs like: `sched-my_function-2025-11-18T10:30:00+00:00`

## Why this matters

1. The column is called `workflow_uuid` but doesn't actually contain UUIDs
2. Creates inconsistency if other workflows use real UUIDs
3. The column name misleads developers about what data to expect

Alternatively, if string IDs are intentional, consider renaming `workflow_uuid` to `workflow_id` throughout the schema to match the actual usage.

title: Allow app schema and system schema to be named separately
body:
# Feature Request: Support Separate Schemas for System and Application Tables

## Problem

Currently, the `dbos migrate` command and `run_dbos_database_migrations()` API only support a single `--schema` parameter. This means both DBOS system tables (workflow_status, operation_outputs, etc.) and application tables must reside in the same PostgreSQL schema.

This limitation prevents users from:
- Organizing databases with different schema naming conventions for infrastructure vs application concerns

## Use Case

Allows for more flexible and customisable separation of application and system database.

## Proposed Solution

Add support for specifying separate schemas for system and application tables:

### CLI
```bash
# Separate schemas
dbos migrate --schema dbos_system --app-schema my_application

# Backward compatible (both use same schema)
dbos migrate --schema my_schema
```

### Programmatic API
```python
run_dbos_database_migrations(
    system_database_url="postgresql://...",
    app_database_url="postgresql://...",
    schema="dbos_system",
    application_schema="my_application"  # NEW optional parameter
)
```

title: Running enqueued/pending workflows on worker startup
body:
Presently, to implement a cron-type worker you need to implement a waiting loop, something similar to the example from the Vercel integration docs (https://docs.dbos.dev/integrations/vercel):

```python
async def main():
    DBOS.launch()
    while True:
        await asyncio.sleep(300)
        if not await DBOS.list_queued_workflows_async():
            break

if __name__ == "__main__":
    asyncio.run(main())
```

Have you considered adding a function (or an optional parameter to `DBOS.launch`) that would wait until all currently enqueued/pending workflows "dequeue-able" by the current worker are executed?

Using a wait loop with `list_queued_workflows` can be bug-prone because you could have a queued workflow in a queue not defined in this worker so this loop would run forever without actually dequeueing any workflows. I encountered this problem myself in Python and solved it by adding the `queue_name` parameter to `list_queued_workflows` (you could maybe adjust the Vercel example so other people don't run into the same issue :).

Or maybe you could extend the Python docs to include an example of this worker "pattern". Curiously, the LLM prompt page has an example of  something related (`await asyncio.Event().wait()`), but this isn't mentioned in the "human-intended" docs, i.e., the case when you have only your workflow code and nothing else.

title: Workflow ID prefix query condition is not properly escaped
body:
The `startswith` condition added to the get workflows query when a `workflow_id_prefix` is set does not escape underscores (`_`) in the provided ID prefix. This leads to dangerously unexpected behaviour (e.g. `workflow_id_prefix='user:1_'` matching a workflow with ID `user:12_34232`).

https://github.com/dbos-inc/dbos-transact-py/blob/85b8073c4933d60ab5e69fac433ed730a9df8281/dbos/_sys_db.py#L1067

The correct way to build a `startswith` query with an unsanitised input is:
```py
query = query.where(
      SystemSchema.workflow_status.c.workflow_uuid.startswith(
          input.workflow_id_prefix, autoescape=True
      )
  )
```

title: Two runners dequeueing from one queue in SQLite sometimes both execute the same enqueued workflow
body:
If you have two runners dequeueing from a single queue it can happen that both of them execute the same enqueued workflow. Happens only with SQLite, didn't encounter this with Postgres.

To reproduce you can use the included example by first enqueuing a lot of workflows and then running two workers simultaneously:

First run the `foo` workflow that enqueues e.g. 1000 `bar` workflows:
```
$ uv run test.py foo 1000
11:18:03 [    INFO] (dbos:_dbos.py:379) Initializing DBOS (v2.7.0a4+g53c42aa)
...
Executing 'foo' workflow 6855405a-8016-4de8-a58b-be9ac65284ca:
- enqueuing 1000 'bar' workflows into 'bar' queue.
- all 'bar' workflows enqueued.
```

Then run two `bar` workers simultaneously (e.g., in separate shells) and you'll end up with more than 1000 executions in total, diff-ing the output you can find out which ones were executed twice:
```
$ uv run test.py bar # worker no. 1
11:18:18 [    INFO] (dbos:_dbos.py:379) Initializing DBOS (v2.7.0a4+g53c42aa)
...
Executing 502. 'bar' workflow (input: 999; id: 6855405a-8016-4de8-a58b-be9ac65284ca-1000).
'bar' workflows executed: 502
```

```
$ uv run test.py bar # worker no. 2
11:18:21 [    INFO] (dbos:_dbos.py:379) Initializing DBOS (v2.7.0a4+g53c42aa)
...
Executing 503. 'bar' workflow (input: 998; id: 6855405a-8016-4de8-a58b-be9ac65284ca-999).
'bar' workflows executed: 503
```

Example code for reproduction (also attached full output of `foo` and `bar` workers: [foo.txt](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/24150098/foo.txt), [bar-1.txt](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/24150099/bar-1.txt) & [bar-2.txt](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/24150097/bar-2.txt)):
```python
import asyncio
import os
import sys

from dbos import DBOS, DBOSConfig, Queue

DBOS_CONFIG: DBOSConfig = {
    "name": "test",
    "system_database_url": os.getenv("DBOS_SYSTEM_DB_URL"),
    "application_version": "0.1.0",
    "executor_id": f"worker-{os.getpid()}",
    "run_admin_server": False,
}

DBOS(config=DBOS_CONFIG)

foo_queue = Queue("foo")
bar_queue = Queue("bar", worker_concurrency=1)

bar_counter = 0


@DBOS.workflow()
async def foo_workflow(num_bars: int) -> None:

    print(f"Executing 'foo' workflow {DBOS.workflow_id}:")

    print(f"- enqueuing {num_bars} 'bar' workflows into 'bar' queue.")
    for i in range(num_bars):
        await bar_queue.enqueue_async(bar_workflow, i)
    print("- all 'bar' workflows enqueued.\n")


@DBOS.workflow()
async def bar_workflow(i: int) -> None:

    global bar_counter
    bar_counter += 1

    print(f"Executing {bar_counter}. 'bar' workflow (input: {i}; id: {DBOS.workflow_id}).")


async def foo_main(num_bars: int) -> None:

    DBOS.listen_queues([foo_queue])
    DBOS.launch()

    await foo_queue.enqueue_async(foo_workflow, num_bars)

    while True:
        await asyncio.sleep(3)
        if not await DBOS.list_queued_workflows_async(queue_name=foo_queue.name):
            break


async def bar_main() -> None:

    DBOS.listen_queues([bar_queue])
    DBOS.launch()

    while True:
        await asyncio.sleep(3)
        if not await DBOS.list_queued_workflows_async(queue_name=bar_queue.name):
            break

    print(f"'bar' workflows executed: {bar_counter}")


if __name__ == "__main__":

    if sys.argv[1] == "foo":
        asyncio.run(foo_main(int(sys.argv[2])))
    elif sys.argv[1] == "bar":
        asyncio.run(bar_main())
    else:
        print("Usage: test.py <foo num_bars|bar>")
```

title: `DBOS` singleton instantiation scope
body:
In all the examples in the docs, the `DBOS` singleton is instantiated in the global scope before any workflow/step functions. Is there a "functional" reason for this?

I tried moving it to a function where `DBOS.launch()` is called and it seems to work OK.

Global scope is problematic when you have multiple workers in different modules and you need to import some workflow function from another worker's module in order to enqueue it (and you keep all the imports at the beginning of the file). The singleton gets instantiated in the module you're importing from and not in the module you're importing to (and you might have defined, e.g., a different executor id).

title: SetWorkflowID does not prevent concurrent execution of workflows with the same ID (until completed)
body:
Thank you for the project - it has been interesting to get to grips with it. I'm not sure if I've found a bug or misunderstood what is supposed to happen here...

## Summary

When multiple concurrent calls use `SetWorkflowID` with the same workflow ID, all of them execute the workflow body in parallel rather than only one executing and the others waiting for/reusing the result.

Idempotency **does work** for sequential calls after a workflow has completed (the cached result is returned without re-execution), but concurrent calls while the workflow is still running all execute the workflow body.

Based on the [Workflow IDs and Idempotency documentation](https://docs.dbos.dev/python/tutorials/workflow-tutorial#workflow-ids-and-idempotency), I expected concurrent calls with the same workflow ID to also be deduplicated.

## Reproduction

```python
import asyncio
from dbos import DBOS, DBOSConfig, SetWorkflowID

config: DBOSConfig = {
    "name": "dbos-test",
    "system_database_url": "postgresql://...",  # your DB URL
    "log_level": "DEBUG",
}
DBOS(config=config)
DBOS.reset_system_database()
DBOS.launch()


@DBOS.workflow()
async def example_workflow():
    DBOS.logger.info(f"START workflow_id={DBOS.workflow_id}")
    await asyncio.sleep(5)
    DBOS.logger.info(f"END workflow_id={DBOS.workflow_id}")
    return {"workflow_id": DBOS.workflow_id}


async def main():
    idempotency_key = "very-unique-id"

    async def call_workflow(n: int):
        print(f"[caller {n}] Starting workflow with ID {idempotency_key}")
        with SetWorkflowID(idempotency_key):
            handle = await DBOS.start_workflow_async(example_workflow)
        result = await handle.get_result()
        print(f"[caller {n}] Got result: {result}")
        return result

    # Test 1: Concurrent calls - all execute the workflow body
    print("\n=== TEST 1: Concurrent calls ===")
    results = await asyncio.gather(
        call_workflow(1),
        call_workflow(2),
        call_workflow(3),
    )
    print(f"Results: {results}")

    # Test 2: Sequential call AFTER completion - idempotency works here
    print("\n=== TEST 2: Sequential call after completion ===")
    result = await call_workflow(4)
    print(f"Result: {result}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    asyncio.run(main())
    DBOS.destroy()
```

## Output

```
=== TEST 1: Concurrent calls ===
[caller 1] Starting workflow with ID very-unique-id
[caller 2] Starting workflow with ID very-unique-id
[caller 3] Starting workflow with ID very-unique-id
[    INFO] START workflow_id=very-unique-id
[    INFO] START workflow_id=very-unique-id
[    INFO] START workflow_id=very-unique-id
[    INFO] END workflow_id=very-unique-id
[    INFO] END workflow_id=very-unique-id
[    INFO] END workflow_id=very-unique-id
[caller 3] Got result: {'workflow_id': 'very-unique-id'}
[caller 1] Got result: {'workflow_id': 'very-unique-id'}
[caller 2] Got result: {'workflow_id': 'very-unique-id'}
Results: [{'workflow_id': 'very-unique-id'}, ...]

=== TEST 2: Sequential call after completion ===
[caller 4] Starting workflow with ID very-unique-id
[caller 4] Got result: {'workflow_id': 'very-unique-id'}
Result: {'workflow_id': 'very-unique-id'}
```

**Test 1:** The workflow body executes 3 times (3 START and 3 END logs).

**Test 2:** No START/END logs - the cached result is returned immediately. Idempotency works here.

## Expected Behavior

I expected only one caller to execute the workflow body in Test 1 as well, with the other two waiting for and returning the result of the already-running workflow.

## Environment

- DBOS version: 2.7.0
- Python: 3.11
- PostgreSQL: 16

## Use Case

We're using DBOS for document processing pipelines where the same document may be requested concurrently by multiple API calls. We use content-based idempotency keys to avoid duplicate processing, but concurrent requests currently all execute the workflow.

title: Dequeued workflows can get stuck in PENDING if execute_workflow_by_id() raises during dispatch
body:
# Summary

I think I've found a bug in the Queue feature that can lead to workflows getting stuck in PENDING without being scheduled for execution by the executor.
This bug can trigger if one of the workflows that an executor dequeues raises an exception while the executor tries to schedule it for execution. 
This will cause the executor to skip scheduling for execution the rest of the dequeued workflows. Which will cause them to get stuck in PENDING.

# Explanation

The bug is located in  [_queue.queue_worker_thread](https://github.com/dbos-inc/dbos-transact-py/blob/f8218e8cb22d37efb241285c65b1c6c787ad9120/dbos/_queue.py#L145) in lines 145 and 138:

```python
            if queue.partition_queue:
                queue_partition_keys = dbos._sys_db.get_queue_partitions(queue.name)
                for key in queue_partition_keys:
                    dequeued_workflows = dbos._sys_db.start_queued_workflows(
                        queue,
                        GlobalParams.executor_id,
                        GlobalParams.app_version,
                        key,
                    )
                    for id in dequeued_workflows:
                        execute_workflow_by_id(dbos, id)
            else:
                dequeued_workflows = dbos._sys_db.start_queued_workflows(
                    queue, GlobalParams.executor_id, GlobalParams.app_version, None
                )
                for id in dequeued_workflows:
                    execute_workflow_by_id(dbos, id)

```

Right after dequeuing new workflows (and claiming them to the executor + setting status to PENDING) in `dbos._sys_db.start_queued_workflows`, each `id`  in `dequeued_workflows` is scheduled for execution on the executor in `execute_workflow_by_id(dbos, id)`.
If `execute_workflow_by_id` were to raise an exception, the for loop will be broken out of and the rest of the workflows won't get scheduled.

# Reproduction

I haven't had the time to test this bug yet, but here is a real world situation where this can occur:

A DBOS client may try to enqueue a workflow that some executors don't have in their `DBOSRegistry`.

```python
from dbos import DBOSClient, EnqueueOptions

client = DBOSClient(system_database_url=os.environ["DBOS_SYSTEM_DATABASE_URL"])

options: EnqueueOptions = {
  "queue_name": "pipeline_queue",
  # Try to enqueue a workflow that the executor won't recognize
  "workflow_name": "workflow_name_that_is_not_in_the_registry",
}
# will cause `dbos._core.execute_workflow_by_id` on the executor to raise `DBOSWorkflowFunctionNotFoundError`,
# which will cause the executor to ignore random workflows that were dequeued with this workflow
handle = client.enqueue(options, task) 
```

If a client were to repeatedly enqueue unregistered workflows, it can cause most workflows on the queue to be stuck in PENDING without being executed on every executor on the queue. Essentially causing Denial of Service.

# Suggested Fix

The simplest fix is to wrap the call to `execute_workflow_by_id` in a `try .. except Exception` that makes sure that all dequeued workflows get a chance to get scheduled for execution.

As to what should be done inside the except block: One option is to call `dbus.logger.warning` with the exception details, which matches the current behavior. This will cause the raising workflow to be stuck in the executor, but at least won't affect the others.
Another, better option is to update the workflow_status table to at least release the executor's lock from the workflow.

I may be unaware to other considerations that may make this harder. I was just browsing the codebase and encountered this.

title: Should be able to use a custom Postgres engine by only specifying system_database_engine
body:
Currently, I need to specify both `system_database_engine` and `system_database_url` if I want to use a custom Postgres engine. Because the default URL is `Defaults to sqlite:///{name} if not provided.`

Therefore, the following code won't work:

```python
my_engine = create_engine('postgresql+psycopg://postgres:dbos@localhost:5432/dbos')

config: DBOSConfig = {
    'name': 'test',
    'system_database_engine': my_engine,
}
DBOS(config=config)
```

The error is caused by DBOS trying to run SQLite queries on the Postgres DB:

```
E           sqlalchemy.exc.ProgrammingError: (psycopg.errors.SyntaxError) syntax error at or near "PRAGMA"
E           LINE 1: PRAGMA foreign_keys = ON
E                   ^
E           [SQL: PRAGMA foreign_keys = ON]
E           (Background on this error at: https://sqlalche.me/e/20/f405)

.venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/psycopg/cursor.py:117: ProgrammingError
```

title: Support concurrent steps
body:
Currently, DBOS assumes that steps don't overlap. Using `asyncio.create_task` to start multiple concurrent steps may lead to incorrect step context/tracing.
We may want to support something similar to https://github.com/dbos-inc/dbos-transact-ts/pull/1051 where steps can be started in parallel and waited later.

An example use case:

```python
  # Create and schedule all steps in parallel
  tasks = [
      asyncio.create_task(process_step(call)) for call in tool_calls
  ]
  ...
  # Wait for results in the same order they started
  for task in tasks:
      res = await task
```

All results must be awaited (think using `asyncio.wait()` with `return_when=asyncio.ALL_COMPLETED`), otherwise, the code can become non-deterministic during replay. `FIRST_COMPLETED` and `FIRST_EXCEPTION` are not deterministic and might not be supported.

title: `workflow_completion_timeout_sec` is not configurable for FastAPI lifespan middleware
Async step's `started_at` and `completed_at` timestamp issue
body: Those two timestamps seem to be exactly the same for async steps. Sync steps are correct.

title: Handle missing notifications in recv/get_event
body:
Today it is possible that a notification from the backend is missed and recv/get_event sleep until the entire timout, even as the awaited value is present in the database.

Consider this scenario:
- Postgres emit a notification on the connection opened with the application
- Postgres crashes before the notification is sent on the wire
- Postgres restart, the notification connection is re-established (by error handling on the application side), but never re-emit the notification
- The application is never notified.

In TypeScript, we handle this by periodically polling Postgres. In Golang, we have a mechanism for the listener loop to signal, through a channel, all the consumers that they should read from the database because the listener connection was lost.

Ideally we'd implement the later in Python.

title: Add configurable internal polling interval for faster test execution
body:
## Problem

Tests run slowly due to hardcoded 1-second polling intervals in internal background threads. During `DBOS.destroy()`, each thread can add up to 1 second of shutdown delay while waiting to notice the stop event. With multiple polling threads (notification listener, queue manager, stream reader), each test cycle loses 2-3+ seconds to shutdown delays.

## Affected polling locations

| Location | File | Description |
|----------|------|-------------|
